topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lilies were offensively bright, their petals a blinding, saccharine yellow against the muted tones of my living room. I stared at the arrangement on the coffee table, the stems perfectly wrapped, a vibrant, almost aggressive display of floral abundance. There was no note. Just the flowers. A slow, tight clench formed in my jaw. I picked up a single, velvety bloom, the texture cool and slick beneath my fingertips, and held it up to the light, examining the pristine perfection as if searching for a flaw. My breath hitched, a shallow, rapid thing in my chest. Who sends something so overtly dramatic without even a courtesy? I dropped the flower back onto the silk cloth, the sound barely audible, and turned sharply toward the front door, my movements stiff and deliberate.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed me the bouquet, the crisp wrapping felt like a deliberate affront. A cascade of deep crimson roses, heavy and unapologetic, was placed in my hands. My fingers brushed against the cool stems, and a wave of profound irritation washed over me, tightening my shoulders almost painfully. I looked down at the empty space where a message should have been, a gaping void in the gesture. I took a step back, my gaze fixed on the flowers as if they were contaminated. "Well," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally flat, "that's certainly… a statement." I didn't wait for a reply, simply turning and walking toward the window, letting the sunlight feel less bright than the blossoms themselves.

[story 3]

I found the flowers resting on my desk this morning, a chaotic jumble of purple irises and stark white carnations. They were too much, too sudden. I picked one up, the stems feeling strangely stiff, and let my thumb trace the unnatural smoothness of the petal. A simmering resentment began to bubble beneath my skin, a hot, prickly sensation behind my eyes. I didn't move to discard them; instead, I placed them precisely in the center of the blotter, making sure they occupied the most prominent space. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, my expression hardening into something cool and unyielding. I waited, sensing the silence in the room was about to become very loud.
